event: Study of the Heads of Saints Thomas and James the Greater
symbols: two apostolic heads in close proximity — the community of disciples sharing in Christ's mission
Thomas's intense gaze — the apostle known for questioning and seeking visible proof
James's downward look — contemplation and the weight of apostolic calling
description: A chalk and charcoal drawing on paper showing two male heads in close proximity. The left face is shown in near-profile, looking to the left with an intense expression, dark curly hair, and a short beard. The right face looks slightly downward in three-quarter view, with longer wavy hair, a beard, and a more contemplative expression. He wears a garment with a round clasp or brooch at the neck. The drawing is warm-toned in red and brown chalks with soft modeling. It has the quality of a preparatory study.
theological_description: Leonardo da Vinci's preparatory study for the Last Supper captures the heads of two apostles — Thomas and James the Greater — in a moment of intense emotional reaction to Jesus's announcement: 'One of you will betray me' (Matthew 26:21). Thomas, shown in sharp profile with a penetrating gaze, is the apostle who would later demand physical proof of the resurrection: 'Unless I see the nail marks in his hands... I will not believe' (John 20:25). Yet when confronted with the risen Christ, Thomas uttered the most complete confession of faith in the Gospels: 'My Lord and my God!' (John 20:28). James the Greater, son of Zebedee and brother of John, was among Jesus's innermost circle — present at the Transfiguration (Mark 9:2), at the raising of Jairus's daughter (Mark 5:37), and in Gethsemane (Mark 14:33). He would become the first apostle to be martyred, killed by Herod Agrippa I (Acts 12:2). Leonardo captures in these faces the full human response to divine revelation — questioning, contemplation, alarm, and devotion — the very emotions that the announcement of betrayal would provoke among those who had left everything to follow Jesus.

scripture_refs.reason: Jesus's announcement of betrayal at the Last Supper — the moment these apostolic reactions capture
Thomas's journey from doubt to the supreme confession 'My Lord and my God'
James the Greater martyred by Herod — the first apostle to die for the faith
James as part of Jesus's inner circle at the Transfiguration

_meta.title: Heads of Sts Thomas and James the Greater
